currentUse: Vacant lot at 416 E. South Street in the Heights section of Wilkes-Barre; a small memorial cross, likely erected by Lithuanians, stands on the empty site per Global True Lithuania's post-demolition observation. No sale of the lot, buyer, or redevelopment has been verified.
summary: Holy Trinity (Švč. Trejybės) Lithuanian Church at 416 E. South Street, Wilkes-Barre — the Gothic-revival "gem on the hill" whose twin steeples dominated the Heights section — was closed by the Diocese of Scranton on May 30, 2010 in the Bishop Martino-era consolidation, with the congregation merged into Our Lady of Hope Parish, Wilkes-Barre. A final Mass in Lithuanian, the first there in roughly half a century, was celebrated in May 2010 by Rev. Vytautas Volertas of New York before about 40 people. Demolition began in mid-January 2015; the diocese stated the lot would be put up for sale once the church was leveled. Its furnishings were dispersed: 14-16 hand-painted 1930s-40s stained-glass windows were salvaged by King Richards Liturgical Design (a crucifixion pair listed at $70,000), the marble altar went to St. Mary's in David City, Nebraska, pews to a Forest City, PA church, and angel statues to nearby parishes and Little Flower Manor. Today the site is an empty lot where a small cross stands, likely erected by Lithuanians. The successor parish, Our Lady of Hope (40 Park Avenue), remains open in the diocese's directory, and Holy Trinity Lithuanian Catholic Cemetery still exists in the Georgetown area near Wilkes-Barre.

developments.headline: Parish closed after a final Mass in Lithuanian; congregation folded into Our Lady of Hope Parish
developments.detail: The Diocese of Scranton's official consolidated-parishes list records Holy Trinity (Lithuanian) Church, Wilkes-Barre as closed 5/30/2010, with Our Lady of Hope Parish, Wilkes-Barre as the receiving parish. The Times Leader reported in May 2010 that a final Roman Catholic liturgy in Lithuanian — the first there in about half a century, and the last — was celebrated by Rev. Vytautas Volertas, who came from New York City, before about 40 people, ahead of the church's scheduled May 30 closing.

developments.headline: Demolition of the century-old church begins
developments.detail: WNEP reported that demolition of Holy Trinity Lithuanian Church on East South Street got underway in mid-January 2015 after weather delays. The church had closed in 2010; the Diocese of Scranton said it planned to sell the property once demolition was complete. Community members came to say goodbye, including a woman whose ancestors helped build the church and whose mother cleaned it for over 40 years and was the last to lock its doors.

developments.headline: Artifacts dispersed nationwide; diocese to list the cleared lot for sale
developments.detail: The Citizens' Voice reported that King Richards Liturgical Design salvaged 14-16 hand-painted stained-glass windows made in the 1930s-40s (a pair depicting the Crucifixion listed at $70,000), the marble altar was relocated to St. Mary's Catholic Church in David City, Nebraska, pews were sold to a church in Forest City, PA, and angel statues went to nearby parishes and Little Flower Manor in Wilkes-Barre. The pipe organ could not be placed due to removal/restoration costs. Demolition was carried out by Stell Enterprises and Hadley Construction; diocese spokesman William Genello said the lot would be put up for sale. Holy Trinity was one of at least 86 churches closed in the Diocese of Scranton in 2009-2015 and one of seven demolished in Luzerne County.

sources.title: Fixtures from demolished Wilkes-Barre church find new homes (reprint)

developments.headline: Site now an empty lot marked by a small memorial cross
developments.detail: Global True Lithuania documents that the Gothic-revival church 'built by Lithuanian hard-earned money' was demolished in 2015 over Lithuanian protests, and that a small lonely cross, likely erected by Lithuanians, now stands in the empty lot. The same source notes the parish's 1935 cemetery survives, with Lithuanian-language inscriptions and a rare monument to Lithuanians who died in World War II.

sources.title: Pennsylvania Northern Coal Region Lithuanian sites

developments.headline: Successor parish Our Lady of Hope remains open
developments.detail: Our Lady of Hope Parish, 40 Park Avenue, Wilkes-Barre — the parish that received Holy Trinity's congregation in 2010 — is still listed as an active parish in the Diocese of Scranton's find-a-parish directory. (Search-level listings suggest it is now led by a deacon serving as Parish Life Coordinator rather than a resident pastor, a possible marker of restructuring exposure, but this was not confirmed on an opened page.)

sources.title: Find a Parish (Our Lady of Hope Parish, Wilkes-Barre)

sources.title: Pennsylvania Northern Coal Region Lithuanian sites
Holy Trinity Church - 416 E South St, Wilkes Barre, PA (address listing)

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: The archive record states "Closed: 2015" — this conflates closure with demolition. The diocese's official consolidated-parishes document records the church as closed 5/30/2010; demolition occurred in early 2015 (began mid-January per WNEP, salvage reported March 2015 per The Citizens' Voice). The archive's "demolished 2015" and "diocese_closed" ending are confirmed. Refinement to "site now a flat field": Global True Lithuania reports a small memorial cross standing in the otherwise empty lot. Additional refinement: the congregation was merged into Our Lady of Hope Parish, Wilkes-Barre (still open), and the parish's 1935 Lithuanian cemetery with a WWII Lithuanian memorial survives. Founding: the congregation dates to 1893 and the demolished building to 1911 per press accounts (search-level, building date not confirmed on an opened page).
gaps: Whether the cleared lot at 416 E. South Street was ever actually sold — buyer, price, and year — could not be verified; the diocese said in 2015 it would list the lot, but no transaction record or news of a sale was found (searched news + property terms; a Redfin page for "416 E South St" describes a small 4,356 sq ft residential parcel, which does not clearly match the church parcel, so it was not used). The current 2026 physical state of the site is unverified beyond Global True Lithuania's undated post-2015 observation of an empty lot with a cross. The Times Leader last-liturgy details (Rev. Vytautas Volertas, ~40 attendees) come from search-result excerpts of the dated article; the archived page body did not render on fetch. WNEP article content likewise came via search excerpts after three fetch timeouts. Our Lady of Hope's leadership/restructuring exposure under Vision 2030 was not confirmed on an opened page. No preservation campaigns, reunions, or recent (2020s) press specific to this parish were found.
